title: Unrecognized Southeast Alaska Native Communities Recognition and Compensation Act
short_title: Unrecognized Southeast Alaska Native Communities Recognition and Compensation Act
plain_summary: This bill helps five Alaska Native villages in Southeast Alaska that were left out when land was given to other Native communities years ago. The villages are Haines, Ketchikan, Petersburg, Tenakee, and Wrangell. Each village can now form a special corporation and will receive about 23,000 acres of federal land. The Native people living in these villages can become owners in their new corporations while still keeping their rights in the larger regional corporation. These new corporations can also create trusts to help their people with health, education, and preserving their Native culture.
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 499.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Unrecognized Southeast Alaska Native Communities Recognition and Compensation Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Purpose
sections.text: The purpose of this law is to fix a problem. Five Alaska Native communities in southeast Alaska were left out when the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act was created. These communities are Haines, Ketchikan, Petersburg, Tenakee, and Wrangell. This law allows Alaska Natives who are enrolled in these communities to form Urban Corporations (special business organizations for Alaska Natives) and to receive certain settlement land under the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act.

sections.title: Section 3 Establishment of Additional Native Corporations
sections.text: This section changes the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act by adding new rules. The Native residents of Haines, Ketchikan, Petersburg, Tenakee, and Wrangell, Alaska can now organize as Urban Corporations. This change does not affect any land rights that other Native Corporations already have.

sections.title: Section 4 Shareholder Eligibility
sections.text: This section changes the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act to add rules about who can own shares in the new Urban Corporations. The Secretary (a government official) must enroll individual Natives into Urban Corporations based on which village they originally enrolled in. Each Native who gets enrolled and who was already a shareholder of the Regional Corporation for Southeast Alaska will receive one hundred shares of Settlement Common Stock in their Urban Corporation. If a Native received shares through inheritance from a deceased Native who originally enrolled in one of these five villages, they will receive the same number of shares in the Urban Corporation. These changes do not affect land allocations to Regional Corporations.

sections.title: Section 5 Distribution Rights
sections.text: This section changes how corporate funds and income are distributed under the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act. It reorganizes existing text and adds a new rule. Native members of Haines, Ketchikan, Petersburg, Tenakee, and Wrangell who become shareholders in an Urban Corporation can still receive distributions as at-large shareholders of the Regional Corporation for Southeast Alaska. The section also states that this new law will not change the ratio for revenue distribution among Native Corporations or affect existing settlement agreements between Regional Corporations or Village Corporations.

sections.title: Section 6 Compensation
sections.text: This section adds a new section to the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act that creates Urban Corporations for Haines, Ketchikan, Petersburg, Tenakee, and Wrangell. The Secretary must give each Urban Corporation the surface rights to approximately twenty three thousand and forty acres of federal land. The specific parcels are shown on detailed maps dated June twenty seven, two thousand twenty five. The Regional Corporation for Southeast Alaska gets the subsurface rights to this same land. The government has two years to complete these land transfers after each Urban Corporation is formed. These land transfers are the full and final payment to these communities under this law. The federal land is withdrawn from other uses until it is transferred. The transferred land must remain open for public hunting, fishing, and recreation, but the Urban Corporations can impose reasonable restrictions for safety and other purposes. Special use permits for guiding and outfitting on the transferred land will be transferred to the Urban Corporations. The Forest Service and Urban Corporations must create mutual use agreements for roads and facilities. Each Urban Corporation may also establish a settlement trust to promote health, education, and welfare in their communities, with proceeds first going to elders and children.
